---
title: "Phase 1 — Corpus Characterization and Problem Scoping"
subtitle: "Telugu OCR via Large Language Models · CSCI/DASC 6020"
author: "Author"
date: "Summer 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    fig-width: 6.5
    fig-asp: 0.6
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
import json, os
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import numpy as np

# Load the statistics generated by corpus_characterize.py
# If outputs/corpus_stats.json doesn't exist yet, we use the known
# values from the dataset card (AlbertoChestnut/telugu-ocr).
STATS_PATH = "../outputs/corpus_stats.json"

KNOWN_STATS = {
    "total_books_in_manifest": 217,
    "approved_books": 217,
    "total_pages_approved": 32949,
    "quality_distribution": {
        "validated": 64,
        "human_proofread": 88,
        "problematic": 19,
        "not_proofread": 46,
        "no_text": 0,
    },
    "telugu_ratio": {
        "mean": "N/A",
        "median": "N/A",
        "min": "N/A",
        "max": "N/A",
        "pct_above_90": "N/A",
    },
    "pages_per_book": {
        "mean": 151.8,
        "median": 116.0,
        "min": 1,
        "max": 915,
    },
}

if os.path.exists(STATS_PATH):
    with open(STATS_PATH) as f:
        stats = json.load(f)
else:
    stats = KNOWN_STATS

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.dpi": 130,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
})
```

## Executive Summary

This report characterizes the Telugu OCR corpus assembled by a teammate from
Telugu Wikisource [@wikisource_telugu] and publicly released as
`AlbertoChestnut/telugu-ocr` on Hugging Face [@chestnut2026telugu]. The
corpus comprises **`{python} stats['approved_books']` books** and
**~`{python} f"{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}"` aligned page pairs**
(scan image + human transcription) totaling approximately 10.3 GB. It provides
a uniquely valuable resource for this project: every page arrives with a
human-authored Telugu transcription from Wikisource's ProofreadPage system,
allowing ground-truth evaluation without additional annotation from scratch.

::: {.callout-note}
**Corpus selection**: The course's corpus guidance [Week 1 announcement]
identifies several acceptable public Telugu OCR sources, including
Wikisource-derived collections. This Wikisource-sourced corpus was selected
as the project's primary dataset on that basis. Supplementary datasets
(e.g. AI4Bharat's Indic OCR resources [@ai4bharat]) may be incorporated in
later phases for additional evaluation diversity.
:::

---

## Corpus Overview

### Source and Provenance

All scan images and transcriptions originate from **Telugu Wikisource**
(`te.wikisource.org`), a community-maintained digital library of Telugu
literature and historical texts. The curation pipeline:

1. Identified Telugu-language PDF books on Wikimedia Commons indexed by
   Wikisource.
2. Filtered to books with ≥ 80% Telugu script characters (measured by
   Unicode block ఀ–౿), ≥ 50 scan pages, and at least one transcribed page.
3. Paired each scan page image (extracted from the source PDF at original
   resolution) with its community transcription.
4. Applied Wikisource's ProofreadPage quality labels to every page.

The resulting dataset is licensed **CC BY-SA 4.0**, permitting unrestricted
academic use.

### Scale and Structure

```{python}
#| label: tbl-overview
#| tbl-cap: "Corpus top-level statistics"

summary = {
    "Metric": [
        "Total books (approved)",
        "Total page pairs (image + text)",
        "Total dataset size",
        "Source",
        "License",
        "Image format",
        "Text encoding",
    ],
    "Value": [
        f"{stats['approved_books']}",
        f"~{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}",
        "~10.3 GB",
        "Telugu Wikisource",
        "CC BY-SA 4.0",
        "JPEG (original scan resolution)",
        "UTF-8 Unicode (NFC)",
    ],
}
pd.DataFrame(summary).set_index("Metric")
```

The on-disk layout is:

```
data/corpus/
  <book_title>/
    page_0001.jpg   ← scan image
    page_0001.txt   ← human transcription (UTF-8)
    page_0004.jpg   ← gaps = pages with no transcription
    page_0004.txt
    meta.json       ← per-book quality and download metadata
  corpus_profile.json ← index of all books with quality and curation details
```

---

## Quality Characterization

### Wikisource ProofreadPage Quality Levels

Each page carries a quality label from the Wikisource ProofreadPage
extension [@proofreadpage]:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-quality-levels
#| tbl-cap: "Wikisource ProofreadPage quality level definitions"

levels = pd.DataFrame({
    "Level": [0, 1, 2, 3, 4],
    "Label": ["no_text", "not_proofread", "problematic", "human_proofread", "validated"],
    "Description": [
        "Blank or image-only page; no transcription present",
        "Transcription exists but has not been reviewed by an editor",
        "Flagged by an editor as containing errors",
        "Reviewed and corrected by one human editor",
        "Reviewed and confirmed by a second independent editor",
    ],
    "Usable as ground truth?": ["No", "With caution", "No (too noisy)", "Yes", "Yes (gold)"],
})
levels.set_index("Level")
```

Levels 3 and 4 together form our **primary ground-truth pool** for CER/WER
evaluation (Phase 4). Levels 1–2 are retained in the full corpus but excluded
from accuracy benchmarking.

### Quality Distribution Across Books

```{python}
#| label: fig-quality-dist
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of average ProofreadPage quality labels across the approved books in the corpus."
#| out-width: "100%"

q = stats["quality_distribution"]
label_order = ["no_text", "not_proofread", "problematic", "human_proofread", "validated"]
counts = [q.get(lbl, 0) for lbl in label_order]
colors = ["#c0392b", "#e67e22", "#f39c12", "#27ae60", "#2980b9"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
bars = ax.bar(label_order, counts, color=colors, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.8, width=0.6)
ax.bar_label(bars, padding=3, fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Average Quality Label (per book)", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Books", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("ProofreadPage Quality Distribution — Approved Books", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)
ax.set_ylim(0, max(counts) * 1.18)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Approximately **`{python} round((q.get('validated',0)+q.get('human_proofread',0))/stats['approved_books']*100)`%** of books carry a mean quality label of *human_proofread* or *validated* — a strong signal that a substantial proportion of transcriptions are reliable enough to serve as direct evaluation ground truth.

### Telugu Script Ratio

The original dataset curation pipeline applied a **≥ 80% Telugu Unicode
block** (ఀ–౿) threshold when selecting books for inclusion, per the
dataset's published methodology. However, the per-book Telugu ratio value
itself was not included in the downloaded `meta.json` metadata, so it
cannot be independently re-verified or visualized from the files available
to us.

::: {.callout-note}
**Known limitation**: `telugu_ratio` is reported as N/A in our generated
statistics because the source metadata does not expose this field. If
exact per-book ratios are needed for Phase 2 filtering decisions, they
would need to be computed directly from the transcription text (counting
Unicode code points in the Telugu block) rather than read from existing
metadata. This is a candidate follow-up task, not a blocker — the curator's
80% inclusion threshold already provides a reasonable assurance that script
contamination is low across the corpus.
:::

---

## Image Resolution and Scan Quality

### Resolution Distribution

The source PDFs were rendered to JPEG at their native scan resolution.
Direct image-level sampling was not run for this report (the
`--skip-images` flag was used to avoid reading all ~32,949 images during
initial characterization); the ranges below are estimates based on
visual inspection of the 40 ground-truth sample pages and typical
Wikisource PDF-to-JPEG export settings, not a full corpus measurement:

| Dimension | Approximate range (estimated) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Width (px) | 1,000 – 2,800 |
| Height (px) | 1,400 – 3,800 |
| Estimated DPI | 150 – 300 DPI (varies by source scan) |
| File size | 150 – 600 KB per page |

::: {.callout-important}
**Action item for Phase 2**: Run `2_corpus_characterize.py` *without*
`--skip-images` to get exact, corpus-wide resolution statistics before
finalizing the preprocessing pipeline. Pages estimated below ~200 DPI
will likely require upscaling before passing to vision-LLM APIs — most
models are optimized for 300 DPI equivalent input. We plan to use
`cv2.resize` with `INTER_CUBIC` for lightweight upscaling, reserving
RealESRGAN for pages where standard upscaling degrades quality.
:::

### Qualitative Scan Artifact Taxonomy

During manual ground-truth annotation (see [Ground Truth Sampling
Strategy](#ground-truth-sampling-strategy) below), every one of the 40
sampled pages was visually inspected and tagged with any scan artifacts
present. These are *observed counts from a 40-page stratified sample*,
not a full corpus census, but they provide a real empirical signal rather
than a guess:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-artifacts
#| tbl-cap: "Scan artifact frequency observed across the 40-page ground truth sample (n=40, multiple tags possible per page)"

artifacts = pd.DataFrame({
    "Artifact Type": [
        "No visible defects (\"Clean\")",
        "Ink fading",
        "Smudging",
        "Blur",
        "Cramped text / tight spacing",
        "Worn / damaged page",
        "Non-text content (illustration)",
        "Non-text content (diagram)",
        "Discoloration",
        "Speckling / noise",
        "Marginalia",
        "Sparse text",
        "Equations",
        "Tiny / small font",
        "No transcription available",
    ],
    "Observed Count (/40)": [19, 18, 9, 7, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1],
    "Preprocessing / Handling Response": [
        "No action needed",
        "Contrast enhancement (CLAHE) + upscaling",
        "Adaptive binarization; background suppression",
        "Bicubic or RealESRGAN upscaling",
        "Careful line-segmentation; avoid over-cropping",
        "Flagging only; manual review for severe cases",
        "LayoutParser region detection; exclude region from text OCR",
        "LayoutParser region detection; exclude region from text OCR",
        "Contrast/white-balance normalization",
        "Morphological despeckling",
        "Flag for manual review; exclude from benchmark",
        "No special handling; verify low character density doesn't bias CER",
        "Flag for manual review (non-Telugu notation)",
        "Upscaling priority candidate",
        "Excluded from Phase 4 benchmark — documented gap, see below",
    ],
})
artifacts.set_index("Artifact Type")
```

Skew, bleed-through, and binding-shadow artifacts — commonly reported in
the broader OCR literature for bound historical texts — were not
explicitly tagged using this vocabulary during annotation, but are
plausible based on visual review and will be checked quantitatively once
full-corpus image sampling (Phase 2) is run.

---

## Telugu Script Challenges

Telugu script poses OCR challenges that go beyond those of Latin or even
most other Brahmic scripts. The following issues are particularly salient
in this corpus.

### Conjunct Characters

Telugu consonants combine into **ligatures** (కూటాక్షరాలు) whose shape
differs substantially from either component character. For instance, the
cluster *k+ṣ* (క + ష) produces the conjunct **క్ష**, a visually distinct
glyph. These conjuncts cannot be reliably recovered by character-level
segmentation strategies — the OCR model must learn to identify the
compound form directly.

**Pipeline implication**: Prompt engineering for vision-LLM OCR should
explicitly instruct the model to output Unicode NFC-normalized sequences
for conjuncts rather than attempting decomposed representations.

### Vowel Diacritics (Mātrās)

Telugu vowel diacritics (ఆ-కారాలు / mātrās) attach to consonants in
spatially complex ways — appearing above, below, before, or after the base
glyph, and sometimes spanning multiple positions. Examples:

- కా (ka + ā-mātrā, appended right)
- కి (ka + i-mātrā, prepended left)
- కు (ka + u-mātrā, below right)
- కూ (ka + ū-mātrā, below, extending further right)

Confusion among mātrās is the single most common OCR error in Telugu
systems [@rao2019telugu]. The ground truth annotation template
(Phase 1 output) includes a `notes` column specifically for recording
mātrā confusion errors during manual inspection.

### Font Diversity

Telugu publishing has employed dozens of typefaces across eras, from the
early Elsevier-style fonts used in colonial-era printing to modern digital
fonts. The Wikisource corpus spans texts from the 19th century onward,
meaning the OCR model encounters substantial typographic variation. This
is a known failure mode for models trained exclusively on modern fonts.

### Low-Resource Context

Despite ~90 million native speakers, high-quality annotated Telugu OCR
datasets remain sparse compared to English, Chinese, or even Hindi. This
makes the Wikisource-derived corpus especially valuable: the community
transcriptions, where quality-level 3 or 4, provide reliable labels
at a scale not readily available elsewhere.

---

## Ground Truth Sampling Strategy

For Phase 4 evaluation, we require a **manually verified** 30–50 page
sample with high-confidence ground truth. We use the following stratified
sampling design:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-gt-strategy
#| tbl-cap: "Ground truth sample allocation by quality level"

gt = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quality Level": ["4 — validated", "3 — human_proofread", "1–2 — lower quality", "0 — no_text"],
    "Target Share": ["40%", "30%", "20%", "10%"],
    "Target Count (n=40)": [16, 12, 8, 4],
    "Rationale": [
        "Gold-standard labels; primary benchmark set",
        "Reliable; expands evaluation coverage",
        "Stress-tests pipeline on noisy inputs",
        "Edge-case handling (blank / image-only pages)",
    ],
})
gt.set_index("Quality Level")
```

The achieved sample differs slightly from this target: the corpus
contains no quality-0 ("no_text") pages, so that allocation was
redistributed, and the sampler's top-up logic shifted some quality-3
slots to fill the gap, yielding 16 validated / 16 human_proofread / 4
problematic / 4 not_proofread pages (see Annotation Results below).

Pages were selected using `3_sample_ground_truth.py` with a fixed random
seed (42) for reproducibility. All 40 pages are stored in
`data/ground_truth/` with paired image and reference text files.

**Manual annotation protocol**: For each sampled page, an annotator:

1. Opened the `.jpg` file and the paired `.txt` (Wikisource transcription).
2. Compared the transcription against the actual page image.
3. Recorded `Confirmed` in `manual_transcription` if the transcription
   matched, or supplied a correction if it did not.
4. Noted artifact types and assigned a subjective scan quality score (1–5).

Annotation was performed jointly using direct visual inspection and an
LLM-assisted comparison workflow (image + transcription pairs reviewed
turn-by-turn) to cross-check character-level accuracy, given the density
of Telugu conjuncts and diacritics.

### Annotation Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-gt-results
#| tbl-cap: "Achieved ground truth sample composition (n=40)"

gt_actual = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quality Level": ["4 — validated", "3 — human_proofread", "2 — problematic", "1 — not_proofread"],
    "Achieved Count": [16, 16, 4, 4],
    "Confirmed Accurate": [16, 16, 3, 4],
    "No Transcription Available": [0, 0, 1, 0],
})
gt_actual.set_index("Quality Level")
```

**Key finding**: Of the 40 sampled pages, **39 had an existing Wikisource
transcription that was confirmed accurate** against the scanned image with
no corrections needed — a strong validation that quality levels 1, 3, and
4 are usable as-is for Phase 4 ground truth. **One page (quality level 2,
"problematic") had no transcription text at all** in the source file,
despite carrying page-level metadata. This is a meaningful finding: not
every page labeled "problematic" merely contains *errors* — some contain
*no transcription whatsoever*. This supports excluding quality level 2
from the Phase 4 benchmark set entirely, rather than treating it as
"noisy but usable."

No corrections were required for any of the 39 transcribed pages in this
sample, which is a notably higher accuracy rate than initially expected
even for quality levels 1 and 2. This may reflect that Wikisource
contributors who initially transcribe a page (level 1) are often
reasonably accurate even before formal proofreading; the "not_proofread"
label reflects review status, not necessarily poor transcription quality.
This is worth re-testing with a larger sample in later phases.

---

## Preprocessing Design Implications

The artifact taxonomy and script challenges above directly motivate the
Phase 2 preprocessing pipeline design:

| Challenge | Phase 2 Response |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Ink fading (observed in 18/40 sampled pages) | Contrast enhancement (CLAHE) + upscaling |
| Smudging / blur (observed in 9 and 7 of 40 pages respectively) | Adaptive binarization; RealESRGAN for severe cases |
| Low-resolution scans (pending full corpus measurement) | Bicubic upscaling to ≥ 300 DPI equivalent before model input |
| Non-text regions (illustrations/diagrams, 5/40 pages) | LayoutParser region detection; exclude from text OCR pass |
| Page skew / binding shadow (not yet quantified) | Deskewing as a universal first step; re-evaluate need after Phase 2 image sampling |
| Mātrā / conjunct confusion | Unicode NFC normalization on all model outputs; prompt instructs model to preserve diacritics |

---

## Summary Statistics

```{python}
#| label: tbl-final-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Complete corpus characterization summary"

rows = {
    "Total books": stats["approved_books"],
    "Total page pairs": f"~{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}",
    "Total size": "~10.3 GB",
    "Estimated total characters": "Pending full text scan (not run with --skip-images)",
    "Pages per book — mean": stats["pages_per_book"]["mean"],
    "Pages per book — median": stats["pages_per_book"]["median"],
    "Pages per book — range": f"{stats['pages_per_book']['min']}–{stats['pages_per_book']['max']}",
    "Telugu script ratio": "N/A in source metadata (curator applied ≥80% threshold)",
    "Books with quality 3–4 label": f"~{round((q.get('validated',0)+q.get('human_proofread',0))/stats['approved_books']*100)}%",
    "Most common observed artifact": "Ink fading (18/40 sampled pages)",
    "Primary script challenge": "Conjunct characters + vowel diacritics",
    "Ground truth sample size": "40 pages (stratified); 39/40 confirmed accurate as-is, 1 had no transcription",
    "Dataset source": "Telugu Wikisource (CC BY-SA 4.0)",
}
pd.DataFrame({"Value": rows}).rename_axis("Metric")
```

---

## AI Tool Usage Disclosure

Per the course's academic integrity policy on LLM-based assistants, the
following tools were used in producing this report and the accompanying
Phase 1 code:

- **Claude (Anthropic)** was used to: scaffold the corpus download,
  profiling, characterization, and ground-truth sampling scripts
  (`0_download_corpus.py` through `3_sample_ground_truth.py`); draft the
  prose and structure of this Quarto report; and assist with debugging
  environment issues (numpy/pandas version conflicts, Unicode encoding,
  HuggingFace rate-limit handling) encountered during setup.
- **LLM-assisted ground truth review**: a subset of the 40 manually
  annotated pages were cross-checked using an LLM-based image-to-text
  comparison workflow (uploading scan image + Wikisource transcription
  pairs) to assist with character-level verification, given the density
  of Telugu conjuncts and diacritics. Final annotation judgments
  (`Confirmed` / correction / no-transcription) were made and recorded by
  the author.
- All generated code was reviewed, run, and debugged by the author and is
  understood in full; all reported statistics were independently
  verified by re-running the corresponding scripts against the
  downloaded corpus.

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
